paintings.title: Shiva Drinking the World-Poison
paintings.location: Plate from Myths of the Hindus & Buddhists (1913)
paintings.story: Shiva drinks the poison churned up from the cosmic ocean to save the world, his throat turning blue — one of Nandalal Bose's celebrated illustrations for Myths of the Hindus & Buddhists (1913), the book written by Sister Nivedita and Ananda K. Coomaraswamy. The book's watercolour illustrations were created under the supervision of Abanindranath Tagore, Bose's teacher, and gave the young painter's mythological imagination an international audience.
funFacts: Sister Nivedita travelled to India in 1898 answering the call of Swami Vivekananda, and wrote numerous books on Indian history, religion and culture.
Bose's classic works include paintings of scenes from Indian mythologies, women, and village life.

paintings.title: Kirat-Arjuna
paintings.location: Plate from Myths of the Hindus & Buddhists (1913)
paintings.story: The Pandava hero Arjuna confronts Shiva disguised as a Kirata hunter, the epic duel that ends with the god granting the hero his divine weapon. Bose painted the scene for Myths of the Hindus & Buddhists, which narrated stories from the Ramayana, Mahabharata, and the lives of Krishna, Buddha and Shiva, illustrated by artists of the new Bengal school under Abanindranath Tagore's supervision.
funFacts: R. Siva Kumar wrote that Bose occupies a place in modern Indian art combining those of Raphael and Dürer in the Renaissance — a great synthesizer with an irrepressible analytical mind.
As a young artist Bose was deeply influenced by the murals of the Ajanta Caves.

paintings.title: The Asceticism of Uma
paintings.location: Plate from Myths of the Hindus & Buddhists (1913)
paintings.story: The goddess Uma stands in solitary penance, undertaking the great austerities by which she won Shiva as her husband. Bose renders her against a bare, luminous ground in the wash manner of the Bengal school. His genius and original style were recognised by artists and critics like Gaganendranath Tagore, Ananda Coomaraswamy and O. C. Ganguli — the circle that founded the Indian Society of Oriental Art.
funFacts: Bose was the first recipient of a scholarship offered by the Indian Society of Oriental Art, founded in 1907.
In 1954 he became the first artist elected Fellow of the Lalit Kala Akademi, India's National Academy of Art.

paintings.title: Yama and Nachiketas
paintings.location: Plate from Myths of the Hindus & Buddhists (1913)
paintings.story: The boy Nachiketas stands before Yama, lord of death, to receive the secret of immortality — the Upanishadic dialogue reimagined for Myths of the Hindus & Buddhists. Bose, who had to persuade a reluctant family to let him study art at Calcutta's School of Art after repeated failures in commerce, was by 1913 among the most gifted of Abanindranath Tagore's pupils.
funFacts: Bose joined an international circle seeking to revive classical Indian culture that included Okakura Kakuzo, William Rothenstein, Yokoyama Taikan and the sculptors Eric Gill and Jacob Epstein.
His mother Khetramoni Devi had a skill in improvising toys and dolls for the young Nandalal.

paintings.title: The Birth of Krishna
paintings.location: Plate from Myths of the Hindus & Buddhists (1913)
paintings.story: Vasudeva carries the newborn Krishna across the storm-swollen Yamuna, sheltered by the hood of the great serpent — one of the most dramatic of Bose's plates for Myths of the Hindus & Buddhists. The stories of Krishna formed one of the book's principal sections, and Bose's rain-lashed night scene shows the Bengal school's atmospheric wash technique bent to epic narrative.
funFacts: The National Gallery of Modern Art in Delhi holds 7,000 of Bose's works in its collection.
Satyajit Ray, the great filmmaker, was among Bose's students at Kala Bhavana.

paintings.title: Garuda
paintings.location: Plate from Myths of the Hindus & Buddhists (1913)
paintings.story: Garuda, the great eagle-mount of Vishnu, spreads his wings in Bose's plate for Myths of the Hindus & Buddhists. Bose was known for his 'Indian style' of painting, forged from the Tagore family's influence and the murals of Ajanta, and mythological beings like Garuda let him unite the decorative force of traditional Indian art with modern draughtsmanship.
funFacts: Nehru asked Bose to sketch the emblems for the Government of India's awards, including the Bharat Ratna and the Padma Shri.
Bose was awarded the Padma Vibhushan in 1954.

paintings.title: Damayanti Svayamvara
paintings.story: Princess Damayanti walks the line of suitors at her svayamvara to choose King Nala — and finds four identical Nalas, the gods having assumed his form to test her. Bose painted the Mahabharata heroine's trial of true sight in the refined manner of the Bengal school. His classic works include paintings of scenes from Indian mythologies, and this subject, beloved of Raja Ravi Varma's generation, is here transformed by the new Indian style.
funFacts: In 1976 the Archaeological Survey of India declared Bose's works among those of the 'nine artists' henceforth considered national art treasures.
Bose became principal of Kala Bhavana, the art college at Tagore's Santiniketan, in 1921.

paintings.title: Agni, the Fire God
paintings.location: Plate from Indian Myth and Legend (1913)
paintings.story: Agni, the Vedic god of fire, rides wreathed in flame in this painting reproduced in Donald Alexander Mackenzie's Indian Myth and Legend (1913), by permission of the Indian Society of Oriental Art, Calcutta. Bose's Vedic and Puranic subjects carried the Bengal school's nationalist project — recovering the gods of India's own imagination for a modern audience.
funFacts: The Indian Society of Oriental Art, which lent this image for publication, had awarded Bose its first scholarship.
Bose decorated the original manuscript of the Constitution of India together with his disciple Beohar Rammanohar Sinha.

paintings.title: The Festival of Cakes
paintings.story: A mother distributes cakes to eager children — possibly the widowed Kunti feeding her five sons, the Pandavas, in exile. The painting was reproduced in The Modern Review in 1914, one of the Calcutta journals that spread the Bengal school's new Indian-style painting to a national readership. Bose's scenes of women and village life stand beside his mythologies among his classic works.
funFacts: To mark Gandhi's 1930 arrest in the Salt March protest, Bose created a black-on-white linocut of Gandhi walking with a staff that became the iconic image of the non-violence movement.
At Gandhi's request Bose made a set of seven posters for the 1938 Haripura session of the Indian National Congress.
